name: Illinois
tooltip: Illinois — Cat 2: Right of Publicity Act plus 2024 digital replica amendments. BIPA-adjacent privacy posture.
rightOfPublicity: Yes — 765 ILCS 1075 (Right of Publicity Act) covers living and deceased persons against unauthorized commercial use of identity, including name, image, likeness, and voice. Statutory damages of $1,000 minimum or actual damages, plus profits and attorney's fees.
postMortem: Yes — 50 years after death. 765 ILCS 1075/30. Transferable and descendible.
aiCoverage: Yes. HB 4875 (P.A. 103-0836, operative Jan. 1, 2025) amended 765 ILCS 1075 to add definitions of "artificial intelligence," "digital replica," and "unauthorized digital replica." HB 4762 (P.A. 103-0830, signed Aug. 9, 2024) is the standalone Digital Voice and Likeness Protection Act, which voids contract provisions allowing creation or use of digital replicas in personal/professional services agreements unless the contract is reasonably specific and the individual was represented by counsel or a labor union.
remedy: Civil suit under 765 ILCS 1075 with $1,000 statutory floor plus profits and fees. For employment contracts, HB 4762 voids overbroad digital replica clauses, giving you renegotiation leverage.
whatsInteresting: Illinois is the second state (after California) to make boilerplate digital-replica contract clauses unenforceable without specific consent and representation. If you're a performer based in Chicago — or signing with a Chicago-based agency, label, or studio — HB 4762 means your standard contract just got friendlier to you. Combined with Illinois's BIPA (Biometric Information Privacy Act, the same statute that gave Facebook a $650M tagging settlement), the state has the most plaintiff-friendly biometric and likeness posture in the country outside California and Tennessee.
primarySources: HB 4762 / P.A. 103-0830 (Digital Voice and Likeness Protection Act) — [ilga.gov](http://ilga.gov)
